author: @travismoore7849
text: I had the stupid idea to get a pip t joint of about 1/2" or an inch and just have the T going into the gas tank of a small engine. Going to the joint is a carbon felt to wick up fuel. The top of the T goes into the tank and the wick could do double duty as a filter. As the engine sucks in air it sucks it through the carbon felt wick to get fuel into the engine. that is the simplest carburetor I could think of.

author: @nigelwilliams7920
text:
So the benefit of this approach appears to be that it provides a way to create water vapour using low-intensity heat, such as from the Sun.  The graphene layer provides a very fine-grained nucleation location where the evaporation occurs.  There are many extant systems which also create steam - such as concentrated solar steam boilers etc.  This device will have application somewhere, but care will be needed to find a niche where it is better than other systems.
In a desalination application (and in a steam generator application) I wonder how this system will get rid of the impurities left behind.  Will they just linger in the sponge layer and eventually clog the interface?  Boiler treatment to remove sludge to prevent scale buildup on interface surfaces is a key item in continuous operating of any boiler.

author: @PressToDigitate
text:
There appear to have been scattered attempts to understand the charge environment at the evaporation interface, and some positive results in both directions; that is, both in extracting a charge consequent to the evaporation and in enhancing the evaporation by manipulating the charge at the gas/liquid boundary layer.  But I'll wager that you're a good bit more clever than those who have looked at it from the standpoint of mere scientific curiosity.  Somehow there will be a configuration of materials, mechanics, thermodynamics and circuit that hits a "sweet spot" of optimum integrated utility.  
On Background:
https://ase.tufts.edu/chemistry/kumar/jc/pdf/Guo_2017.pdf

http://www.electrostatics.org/images/2018_P22.pdf
https://link.springer.com/article/10.1134/S1063784218040205
http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/interfacial_water.html
https://www.mdpi.com/1996-1073/12/1/11/pdfhttps://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/a26c/a0427bfc085b8caf58b21af162fbc573cf17.pdf
https://www.nature.com/articles/srep17276
https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.04543.pdf
https://www.achrnews.com/articles/84092-evaporation-with-electrostatics
https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/8048045
https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166128009001420
http://www.freepatentsonline.com/5565067.html

author: @technosaurus3805
text:
I just use a black, quick dry, micro fiber cloth and a cheap Fresnel lens and get some great results.  I 3d printed a regenerative heat exchanger that preheats the influent while condensing the effluent.  That goes to a collapsible container that draws a vacuum as it fills up.

It may work better with some active carbon to increase the surface area, but I like being able to just toss the cloth in the wash.

replies.author: @mp77744
replies.text: technosaurus is your heat exchanger plastic?  Does it get good heat transfer?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one mate

replies.author: @technosaurus3805
replies.text:
@mp77744 Its 99% plastic. Basically 2 Tesla valves separated by a thin strip of metal.  It doesn't need to be Tesla's valves though - I only used them to minimize  backflow through the influent filter (prefiltering extends the time before needing to wash the microfiber and can be used separately if the water is chemical free)
Because of the limited length  (I wanted to be able to fit it in a backpack) the prototype is only about 80% efficient, but I could get past 90% with a few minor modifications without increasing the size much.

@ThinkingandTinkering Just making a Graphene paint Felt Heat pad :) Again we have success!! So we took an Absorbent wipe and painted it with Graphene paint. I took a resistance and got conductivity across the material. So then I sew on copper tape and applied 20VDC. Now I have a heating pad pushing out 38 Deg C :)

Next step with this is testing with 12VDC and making a larger heat pad

replies.author: @ZhioN360
replies.text: You can collect with a pyramid-shaped cover. If it is an inverted pyramid (upside down) then the water will condense on the cover and gravity will pull the water to the point of the pyramid. Just put a bowl or bucket or something underneath to catch the water that falls. If you have an upright pyramid (a round shape will work too) then gravity will pull the water to the base edges of the pyramid and you can collect there. There are videos on YouTube for solar desalination setups, if you need links just let me know.

replies.author: @eriknielsen1849
replies.text: @ZhioN360 fine for a couple of liters but not 100 m3 a day. But thanks anyway

replies.author: @ZhioN360
replies.text: @eriknielsen1849 Whoa! At that scale you'll definitely need some kind of integrated system. I don't know where you would get that much salt water unless you pumped it straight out the ocean, in which case you need to feed into a desalination chamber(s) and then into a fairly large storage tank system? With Robert's approach here you could desalinate way faster than just with sunlight, so you could get through an order of magnitude more water in the same amount of time. Anyway, all the best of luck

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @VinoVeritas_ I was going to say, I just painted a flask with flat black spray and peel and then put a mylar mirror space blanket on a frame around my liebig condenser, (metal side toward the sun, to reflect all that thermal energy along with everything else, away from it, so tested the surface for the conductive metal coating first) with no solar concentration and it still worked quite well, I think people forget that if you evaporate something in a hurry so that you get higher pressure on the other side, the pressure forces out the heat faster so that it can return to a liquid at least, even if it is not getting outright cold, just hot water, that will cool off on its own, the top was still clear borosilicate glass so getting plenty of UV from the sun to sterilize things. the real trick would be ensuring that no gases trapped in the water get transferred and absorbed into the water again.

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: I should point out too, any flat black substance would work, so those with no money could use burnt stuff to make soot on plastic bottles from soda or something, and any type of tubing they can clean up to ensure it is safe to drink from when the water condenses, and for the silver reflector to keep the condensers at air temp rather than being heated by the sun, they can use potato chip (crisps I guess if you are in the UK) bags that have those mylar like linings, even if it is not the metal on the surface, and some coat hangers or what ever to make the frame around the coil or tubes making clean drinking water in a decently controlled environment possible even where only stuff thrown away can be had. basically doing it Mad Max style XD

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yep - it is funny but some folks have missed that
